tldr: Headlines across 10 buckets converge on the economic fact of a US 25% tariff on Brazilian imports, but split on emphasis: most outlets use neutral verbs for the action, several foreground the 'unfair trade practices' justification, the Guardian uniquely leads with Brazil's condemnation, and SCMP alone frames the dispute as a Rubio–Lula political confrontation. The dominant signal is economic action with a fairness sub-narrative; political and diplomatic frames remain isolated outliers.

evidence.quote: Trump imposes 25% tariffs on Brazilian goods
US launches new round of tariffs with 25% tax
Usa impongono dazi del 25% su alcuni prodotti

frames.sub_frame: unfair trade practices as tariff rationale

evidence.quote: US announces new 25% tariffs on Brazil for 'unfair'
Brazil condemns US move to impose 25% tariffs next week
US slaps 25% tariffs on Brazilian imports

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only headline to personalize the tariff dispute through named politicians, framing it as Rubio attributing the tariffs to Lula's 'ego' — the most politically charged headline in the corpus and the only one to introduce leader-level agency.

Sole headline to lead with Brazil's reaction ('condemns') rather than the US action, repositioning the news subject from sender to recipient of the tariff — the only title to grant Brazil grammatical primacy.

Uses 'slaps' instead of 'imposes' or 'sets', the only informal/aggressive verb in the corpus, and alone adds 'as trade tensions escalate' to foreground bilateral conflict escalation beyond the tariff announcement itself.

bottom_line: The headline layer frames this as a US-initiated economic action against Brazil, with the 25% tariff figure appearing in every title, a fairness/legitimation sub-narrative ('unfair trade practices') cutting across multiple outlets, and political personalization confined to a single HK outlet.
